name: Nathamuni
dates_notes: Sectarian chronicles only; foundational figure who recovered the Āḻvārs' Tamil hymns.
core_thesis: Nāthamuni's surviving works are lost; almost everything attributed to him is reconstructed from later citation. What can be retrieved is a programme rather than a doctrine. The programme has three components. First, the recovery of the Tamil *prabandham* of the *Āḻvārs* — *Tiruvāymoḻi* and the rest — and its placement alongside the Sanskrit *śruti* corpus as authoritative for Vaiṣṇava theology. Second, the integration of Pāñcarātra liturgical theology with *Brahma-Sūtra* exegesis, so that temple-worship of Nārāyaṇa-with-Śrī is treated as continuous with Vedāntic *upāsanā* rather than as a parallel non-Vedic cult. Third, the epistemological claim — preserved chiefly through Yāmuna's citations of the lost *Nyāya-Tattva* — that determinate cognition (*saviśeṣa-jñāna*, awareness with content and structure) is veridical, and that the *jīva* is a real, atomic, knowing self (*jñātṛ*) and not a momentary or undifferentiated *cit*. The constructive Viśiṣṭādvaita position — Brahman *qualified by* the world of *cit* and *acit* — is in Nāthamuni only in seed; its philosophical articulation belongs to Yāmuna and Rāmānuja. What Nāthamuni supplies is the institutional and textual ground on which they build: a bilingual liturgical-philosophical corpus with the Tamil hymns at one end and Pāñcarātra-Vedānta at the other.

engaged_works.title: Nyaya-Tattva (lost)
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Work is lost; cited in later Śrīvaiṣṇava tradition only.
engaged_works.summary: A lost *prakaraṇa* on *pramāṇa*, reconstructible only from citations in Yāmuna's *Siddhi-Trayam*, Rāmānuja, and Veṅkaṭanātha. The fragments cluster around two theses. First, that cognition is constitutively *saviśeṣa* — awareness comes always with content, structure, and qualification, never as bare unqualified *cit*. Hence Buddhist *vijñāna-vāda* (consciousness alone, content as projection) and any monism that takes pure *cit* as a self-given datum mistake the phenomenology: there is no grasping of unqualified being. Second, the theory of error: the *Nyāya-Tattva* appears to reject *anyathā-khyāti* (cognising x as y) and *asat-khyāti* (cognising the non-existent) in favour of *yathārtha-khyāti*, on which every cognition has a real object and error consists in partial apprehension rather than in the cognising of a non-entity. The position is the one Rāmānuja inherits in the *Vedārtha-Saṃgraha*. The fragments matter because they are the earliest preserved articulation of the epistemological premise from which Viśiṣṭādvaita derives the metaphysical claim that Brahman must be construed as *viśiṣṭa* (qualified) rather than *nirviśeṣa*: if no cognition is of an unqualified content, the supposed cognition of unqualified Brahman is not a cognition at all. The loss of the full text is one of the most consequential in Vedānta historiography.

engaged_works.title: Yoga-Rahasya (lost)
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Work is lost; ascription is sectarian.
engaged_works.summary: A lost esoteric manual on contemplative practice, ascribed to Nāthamuni in Śrīvaiṣṇava sectarian chronicles and cited in later Maṇipravāḷa rahasya-literature. The reconstructible content has two centres of gravity. The first is *dhruvānusmṛti* (unbroken meditative remembrance) of Nārāyaṇa, treated as the operative form of the *upāsanā* enjoined by *Chāndogya* 7 and *Taittirīya* 2 — the contemplative *vidyā*-passages of the Upaniṣads. The second is the appropriation of *aṣṭāṅga-yoga* into a theistic frame in which the goal is not Patañjali's isolation (*kaivalya*) of *puruṣa* from *prakṛti* but conscious participation in Bhagavān. The doctrinal payoff for the lineage is the equation, central to Yāmuna and Rāmānuja, of *bhakti* with *upāsanā* — devotional remembrance is itself a form of enjoined meditative knowledge, and its maturation is *mokṣa*. The *Yoga-Rahasya* is, on this reconstruction, the missing bridge between Pāñcarātra ritual theology and the Vedāntic *upāsanā*-doctrine that the school's later systematisers presuppose.
